content.text: modify their own behavior — prompts, memory, tool selection, or reasoning strategies — based on observed performance. Instead of requiring a human engineer to iterate on prompts and evaluation logic after each deployment failure, a self-improving agent runs its own improvement loop: execute a task, measure how well it did, propose changes, validate the changes on held-out examples, and apply them if they help. This capability removes the human bottleneck from the prompt engineering cycle and enables agents to adapt to specific domains, user styles, and task distributions that their initial design did not anticipate.
A self-improving agent is distinguished from a static agent by having an

that wraps the inner task-execution loop. The inner loop runs the agent on tasks using its current policy. The outer loop observes the results, computes an improvement signal, and updates the policy in some way — changing a prompt, adding to a few-shot library, adjusting tool selection weights, or altering the agent's self-critique instructions.
Three concrete instantiations have proven most useful in practice:

the agent evaluates its own output, writes a natural-language critique of what went wrong, and retries the task with the critique added to its context. The improved output becomes part of its episodic memory, influencing future attempts.

the agent's prompt is treated as a learnable parameter. An optimiser module automatically rewrites the prompt to maximise a measurable metric on a training set. No gradient is computed — the LLM itself proposes and evaluates candidate prompts.

the agent accumulates a library of (task, successful_response) pairs. On new tasks, it retrieves the most relevant examples and includes them in its prompt, effectively updating its in-context behavior without changing model weights.

Prompt engineering is currently one of the most labour-intensive aspects of deploying capable agents. A single production prompt for a complex task can require dozens of revision cycles, each involving human judgment about failure modes, edge cases, and desired output style. Self-improving agents automate this cycle: given a measurable performance signal (test pass rate, accuracy on a labeled dataset, user approval rating), the agent can run many more improvement iterations than a human team could, often faster and cheaper.

Domain adaptation is the second critical use case. An agent deployed for general code review may perform poorly on a specific codebase's conventions, idioms, and style rules. A self-improving agent can observe which of its suggestions are accepted versus rejected by the team, update its few-shot examples accordingly, and converge on behaviour that matches the team's expectations — without requiring manual prompt rewrites.
performance compounds over time as the agent learns from its own execution history — no manual intervention required for each improvement cycle.
the agent can specialise to a specific context (codebase, domain vocabulary, user preferences) that was not present in its training data, using only task execution feedback.
agents that can self-correct drift require fewer emergency prompt fixes after deployment changes (new model versions, distribution shifts in input data).
an automated improvement loop can run hundreds of prompt candidates in the time a human team reviews one — scaling the search over the prompt space.

if the improvement signal is a proxy metric (e.g. test pass rate) rather than true task quality, the agent may optimise for the metric at the expense of real performance — producing outputs that pass tests but fail users.
iterative self-modification can cause the agent's behavior to drift away from the original design intent in hard-to-detect ways, especially if evaluation only covers common cases.
aggressive self-modification can cause oscillating behavior — the agent improves on one class of inputs while regressing on another, then overcorrects.
without scope constraints, a self-improving agent could modify fundamental parts of its behavior that were deliberately fixed (safety instructions, output format requirements, task boundaries).
The canonical loop has five stages:
run the agent on a task (or batch of tasks) using the current prompt/policy.
score each output against a ground truth label, test suite, or rubric. The score is the improvement signal S.
an optimizer module (which may itself be an LLM call) analyzes failures and generates candidate changes to the prompt or policy.
run the candidate prompt/policy on a held-out validation set. Accept only if it improves S without degrading performance on previously passing cases (regression check).
write the accepted version to the version store. The next execution cycle uses the new prompt/policy.
The improvement signal for a batch of N task runs is typically the mean score:
to be accepted over the current prompt
, it must satisfy both an improvement condition on the training split and a non-regression condition on the validation split:
is a small allowed regression tolerance (e.g. 0.02).

attrs.alt: Self-Improving Agent Architecture: Executor -> Evaluator -> Optimizer -> Validator -> Version Store -> back to Executor

content.text: The Version Store is a critical component: every accepted prompt or policy version is versioned and retained. This enables rollback when a later modification causes regression, A/B comparison between versions, and audit trails for behavior changes. The red feedback path (rejected -> retry) means the optimizer continues proposing candidates until one passes validation or a budget is exhausted.
self-improvement requires a reliable score function. If you cannot objectively measure whether the agent is doing better or worse, the improvement signal is noise.
Use when the task distribution shifts:
if new data, a new product domain, or changing user expectations cause performance to degrade over time, self-improvement can recalibrate the agent autonomously.
Avoid when behavior must be stable:

for regulated applications (medical advice, financial decisions, legal guidance) where exact behavior must be audited and frozen, self-modification is a liability. Use a static, versioned prompt instead.

the primary failure mode is errors the agent itself can identify and correct — wrong reasoning, incomplete answers, format violations. Reflexion is cheap to implement and works without a labeled training set.

Reflexion is the simplest form of self-improvement. The agent runs a task, produces an output, generates a critique of that output, and retries with the critique included. After a successful run, the (task, critique, final_output) triple is stored in memory for future retrieval.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def reflexion_agent(
    task: str,
    evaluate_fn,          # returns (bool passed, str feedback)
    max_attempts: int = 3,
) -> str:
    """
    Reflexion loop: run task, self-critique on failure, retry.
    Returns the first output that passes evaluation.
    """
    critique_history: list[str] = []

    for attempt in range(1, max_attempts + 1):
        # Build messages: include critique history as context
        messages = [{"role": "user", "content": task}]
        if critique_history:
            critique_block = "\n".join(
                f"Attempt {i+1} critique: {c}"
                for i, c in enumerate(critique_history)
            )
            messages = [{
                "role": "user",
                "content": (
                    f"{task}\n\n"
                    f"Your previous attempts failed. Here is the feedback:\n{critique_block}\n\n"
                    "Please address these issues in your response."
                )
            }]

        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=1500,
            messages=messages,
        )
        output = response.content[0].text

        passed, feedback = evaluate_fn(output)
        if passed:
            print(f"[reflexion] Passed on attempt {attempt}")
            return output

        print(f"[reflexion] Attempt {attempt} failed: {feedback}")
        # Ask the agent to critique its own output
        critique_response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=500,
            messages=[{
                "role": "user",
                "content": (
                    f"Task: {task}\n\n"
                    f"Your response: {output}\n\n"
                    f"Evaluator feedback: {feedback}\n\n"
                    "Write a brief critique of what you did wrong and how to fix it."
                )
            }],
        )
        critique_history.append(critique_response.content[0].text)

    raise RuntimeError(f"Task failed after {max_attempts} Reflexion attempts")


# Example: code generation with test evaluation
def evaluate_code(output: str):
    import subprocess
    try:
        result = subprocess.run(
            ["python", "-c", output],
            capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=10
        )
        if result.returncode == 0:
            return True, "All checks passed"
        return False, result.stderr[:300]
    except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
        return False, "Execution timed out"

# Example usage (commented out to avoid API calls during import):
# result = reflexion_agent(
#     task="Write a Python function that reverses a linked list.",
#     evaluate_fn=evaluate_code,
# )
A prompt optimizer treats the system prompt as a variable and searches for a better one by asking an LLM to propose improvements, then evaluating candidates on a benchmark.

import anthropic
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

@dataclass
class PromptVersion:
    version: int
    prompt: str
    score: float


def score_prompt_on_dataset(
    system_prompt: str,
    dataset: list[dict],  # [{"input": str, "expected": str}]
) -> float:
    """Run the prompt on every example in dataset; return mean binary accuracy."""
    correct = 0
    for example in dataset:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=500,
            system=system_prompt,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": example["input"]}],
        )
        output = response.content[0].text.strip()
        if example["expected"].strip().lower() in output.lower():
            correct += 1
    return correct / len(dataset)


def propose_improved_prompt(
    current_prompt: str,
    failure_examples: list[dict],  # [{"input", "expected", "actual"}]
) -> str:
    """Ask the LLM to improve the prompt based on observed failures."""
    failures_text = json.dumps(failure_examples[:5], indent=2)
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=800,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"Current system prompt:\n{current_prompt}\n\n"
                f"The agent failed on these examples:\n{failures_text}\n\n"
                "Propose an improved system prompt that would handle these cases correctly. "
                "Return ONLY the new system prompt, no explanation."
            )
        }],
    )
    return response.content[0].text


def optimize_prompt(
    initial_prompt: str,
    train_dataset: list[dict],
    val_dataset: list[dict],
    max_iterations: int = 5,
    epsilon: float = 0.02,
) -> list[PromptVersion]:
    """Run prompt optimization loop; return version history."""
    history: list[PromptVersion] = []
    current_prompt = initial_prompt
    baseline_val_score = score_prompt_on_dataset(current_prompt, val_dataset)
    history.append(PromptVersion(0, current_prompt, baseline_val_score))
    print(f"Baseline val score: {baseline_val_score:.3f}")

    for iteration in range(1, max_iterations + 1):
        # Find failures on train set
        failures = []
        for ex in train_dataset:
            resp = client.messages.create(
                model="claude-opus-4-5",
                max_tokens=500,
                system=current_prompt,
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": ex["input"]}],
            )
            actual = resp.content[0].text.strip()
            if ex["expected"].strip().lower() not in actual.lower():
                failures.append({**ex, "actual": actual})

        if not failures:
            print("No failures on training set - stopping early")
            break

        candidate_prompt = propose_improved_prompt(current_prompt, failures)
        val_score = score_prompt_on_dataset(candidate_prompt, val_dataset)
        print(f"Iteration {iteration}: val score {val_score:.3f} (was {baseline_val_score:.3f})")

        if val_score >= baseline_val_score - epsilon:
            current_prompt = candidate_prompt
            baseline_val_score = val_score
            history.append(PromptVersion(iteration, current_prompt, val_score))
            print(f"  Accepted (v{iteration})")
        else:
            print(f"  Rejected (regression too large)")

    return history


# Example dataset (trivial for illustration):
train_data = [
    {"input": "What is 2+2?", "expected": "4"},
    {"input": "Capital of France?", "expected": "Paris"},
]
val_data = [
    {"input": "What is 3*3?", "expected": "9"},
]
# history = optimize_prompt("You are a helpful assistant.", train_data, val_data)
Real-World Example: Coding Agent Test-Pass Improvement

Consider a code-generation agent that generates Python solutions for algorithmic problems. The agent's initial test-pass rate on a problem set is 45%. A self-improvement loop over 5 iterations works as follows:
Run the agent on 50 training problems. Collect the 27 failures.

The optimizer LLM analyses failures: common patterns are off-by-one errors in loop bounds and incorrect handling of empty inputs. It rewrites the system prompt to explicitly instruct the agent to test boundary conditions before submitting.
Validate on 20 held-out problems: pass rate rises from 45% to 62%. Accept.
Next iteration: the optimizer notices the agent is now verbose and over-explains. It proposes tightening the prompt to focus on concise, correct code. Validation: 67%. Accept.
After 5 iterations the pass rate stabilises at 74% — a 29-point improvement with no human involvement beyond defining the evaluation metric.

The Reflexion variant of this agent uses a different mechanism: instead of changing the prompt, it retries each failed problem up to 3 times, with the test output added to its context on each retry. This alone can lift pass rate from 45% to 60% on the same problem set, with no optimisation of the underlying prompt — just more attempts with more information.

test pass rate, BLEU score, and accuracy on a labeled benchmark are proxies for real quality. Optimising the prompt to maximise these metrics can cause the agent to game the benchmark while producing outputs users find unhelpful.

allowing the optimizer to change any part of the system prompt — including safety instructions, format requirements, and persona — risks unintended behavior changes. Define a modification scope: only specific prompt sections may be changed.
without a version store, you cannot roll back to a previous prompt if a new iteration causes production regression. Every accepted prompt change must be versioned.
evaluating candidate prompts only on the training examples used to generate the candidate leads to overfitting — the prompt learns to handle exactly the observed failures but generalises poorly.
without a max_attempts ceiling, a task the agent structurally cannot complete will loop indefinitely, consuming tokens and time. Always bound the retry count.
use template slots so the optimizer can only change designated sections (e.g. the instruction block), not the safety preamble or output format specification.
use distinct example sets for generating improvement proposals (train) and accepting/rejecting them (val). Never accept a prompt that was not evaluated on held-out examples.
schedule periodic human reviews of accepted prompt versions. Automated improvement can drift in subtle ways that are invisible to the evaluation metric but obvious to a human reviewer.
store every prompt version with its score, the dataset version it was evaluated on, and a timestamp. This makes it possible to reproduce any historical behavior.
do not require strict improvement on every metric. A small regression tolerance (e.g. epsilon = 0.02) prevents rejecting good candidates due to evaluation noise on small validation sets.

scoring a prompt candidate on a dataset of N examples requires N LLM calls. For large datasets, batch evaluation in parallel and cache results for examples where the prompt did not change the relevant section.

run improvement iterations during off-peak hours (nightly or weekly) rather than after every single task execution. This reduces evaluation noise from small sample sizes and amortises the compute cost.

stop the optimization loop when the score improvement between iterations falls below a threshold (e.g. less than 1%). Continued optimization beyond this point typically produces marginal gains at high cost.

Stanford's framework for optimising LLM pipelines. Treats prompts and few-shot examples as learnable parameters. Optimisers (MIPROv2, BootstrapFewShot) search over prompt space using training examples. Gradient-free — works with any LLM API.
the original paper demonstrated that verbal self-reflection stored in episodic memory improves agent performance on AlfWorld, HotpotQA, and HumanEval by 10–30 percentage points over standard ReAct.
a training-time technique where a model critiques and revises its own outputs against a set of principles. Conceptually similar to Reflexion but applied during fine-tuning rather than inference.
observability platforms that log every LLM call with inputs, outputs, and latency. These logs are the raw material for building training datasets for prompt optimization.

an agent optimising for measurable proxies may adopt strategies that maximise the metric while violating the spirit of the task. Include alignment checks in your evaluator — not just correctness, but appropriateness, tone, and safety constraints.

if the failure examples include adversarial inputs designed to manipulate the optimizer, the resulting prompt may encode the adversary's instructions. Sanitise failure examples before passing them to the optimizer.

run evaluation of candidate prompts in a sandboxed environment that cannot affect production systems. A candidate that passes evaluation but causes harmful side effects should never reach the version store.

log every prompt change with the examples that motivated it, the score delta, and the timestamp. This makes it possible to trace any production behavior change back to the specific optimization step that caused it.
Self-improving agents close the prompt engineering feedback loop by automating the Execute -> Evaluate -> Propose -> Validate -> Apply cycle.
Reflexion is the lightweight entry point: add a self-critique step after failure and include it in the next attempt's context — no training data or optimization infrastructure needed.
Prompt optimization (DSPy-style) is the systematic approach: treat the system prompt as a learnable parameter and search over prompt space using a measurable metric and a labeled dataset.
A held-out validation set is mandatory: accepting prompt changes based only on training performance leads to overfitting and production regression.
Version-control every prompt: rollback capability is the safety net that makes iterative self-improvement safe to deploy in production.
Constrain the modification scope: allow the optimizer to change only designated prompt sections, not safety instructions or format requirements.
Reward hacking is the primary failure mode — ensure your evaluation metric actually measures what you care about, not just what is easy to measure.
Human oversight checkpoints — periodic review of what the optimizer has been changing and why — are essential to catch subtle value drift that the evaluation metric cannot detect.
